concepts.name: Classification of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
concepts.explanation: Haloalkanes contain halogen atom(s) attached to sp³ hybridised carbon of an alkyl group. Haloarenes contain halogen atom(s) attached to sp² hybridised carbon of an aryl group. They are classified as mono, di, or polyhalogen based on the number of halogen atoms.

concepts.name: Alkyl Halides – Primary, Secondary, Tertiary
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides are classified based on the nature of the carbon to which halogen is attached: primary (1° – attached to one carbon), secondary (2° – attached to two carbons), and tertiary (3° – attached to three carbons). Examples: CH₃Cl (1°), CH₃CH(Cl)CH₃ (2°), (CH₃)₃CCl (3°).

concepts.name: Allylic and Benzylic Halides
concepts.explanation: Allylic halides have halogen attached to a sp³ carbon adjacent to a C=C bond (e.g., CH₂=CH–CH₂Cl). Benzylic halides have halogen attached to a sp³ carbon adjacent to an aromatic ring (e.g., C₆H₅–CH₂Cl).

concepts.name: Vinylic and Aryl Halides
concepts.explanation: Vinylic halides have halogen attached to a sp² carbon of a C=C bond (e.g., CH₂=CHCl). Aryl halides have halogen directly bonded to a sp² carbon of an aromatic ring (e.g., C₆H₅Cl).

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
concepts.explanation: Haloalkanes are named as halo-substituted alkanes (e.g., 2-chlorobutane). Haloarenes are named as halo-substituted benzenes (e.g., chlorobenzene). For dihalo derivatives, o/m/p (common) or 1,2/1,3/1,4 (IUPAC) are used. Geminal halides have halogens on the same carbon; vicinal halides have halogens on adjacent carbons.
prerequisites: OC_13 (IUPAC nomenclature of branched chain alkanes)

concepts.name: Nature of C-X Bond – Bond Length, Bond Enthalpy, Dipole Moment
concepts.explanation: The C-X bond is polarised due to higher electronegativity of halogen. Bond length increases down the group: C-F < C-Cl < C-Br < C-I. Bond enthalpy decreases down the group: C-F > C-Cl > C-Br > C-I. Dipole moments: CH₃Cl (1.86 D), CH₃Br (1.83 D), CH₃I (1.64 D).

concepts.name: Preparation of Haloalkanes – From Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Alcohols react with halogen acids (HX), phosphorus halides (PX₃, PX₅), or thionyl chloride (SOCl₂) to form haloalkanes. SOCl₂ is preferred as by-products are gaseous. Reactivity order: 3° > 2° > 1°. Example: ROH + SOCl₂ → RCl + SO₂ + HCl.

concepts.name: Preparation of Haloalkanes – From Hydrocarbons
concepts.explanation: Alkanes undergo free radical halogenation (Cl₂ or Br₂, hv) to give haloalkanes. Alkenes add HX (Markovnikov rule) or halogens (X₂) to give haloalkanes. Example: CH₄ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + HCl (hv); CH₂=CH₂ + Br₂ → CH₂Br–CH₂Br.
concepts.formula_reaction: RH + X₂ → RX + HX (hv); RCH=CH₂ + HX → RCH(X)CH₃

concepts.name: Preparation of Haloalkanes – Finkelstein Reaction
concepts.explanation: Alkyl chlorides or bromides react with NaI in dry acetone to form alkyl iodides. NaCl or NaBr precipitates, driving the reaction forward. Example: R-Br + NaI → R-I + NaBr (dry acetone).
concepts.formula_reaction: R-X + NaI → R-I + NaX (X = Cl, Br; dry acetone)

concepts.name: Preparation of Haloalkanes – Swarts Reaction
concepts.explanation: Alkyl chlorides or bromides react with metallic fluorides (AgF, Hg₂F₂, CoF₂, SbF₃) to form alkyl fluorides. Example: CH₃Br + AgF → CH₃F + AgBr.

concepts.name: Preparation of Haloarenes – Electrophilic Substitution
concepts.explanation: Aryl chlorides and bromides are prepared by halogenation of arenes with Cl₂ or Br₂ in presence of Lewis acid catalyst (Fe or FeCl₃). Example: C₆H₆ + Cl₂ → C₆H₅Cl + HCl (FeCl₃).

concepts.name: Preparation of Haloarenes – Sandmeyer's Reaction
concepts.explanation: A diazonium salt (from primary aromatic amine) reacts with cuprous chloride or bromide to give aryl halides. Example: C₆H₅N₂⁺Cl⁻ + CuCl → C₆H₅Cl + N₂ + CuCl₂.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArN₂⁺X⁻ + CuX → ArX + N₂ (X = Cl, Br)

concepts.name: Preparation of Haloarenes – From Diazonium Salts (Iodination)
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts react with KI to give aryl iodides without a catalyst. Example: C₆H₅N₂⁺Cl⁻ + KI → C₆H₅I + N₂ + KCl.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
concepts.explanation: Boiling points: RCl < RBr < RI (same alkyl group); branching decreases b.p.; haloarenes have higher b.p. than corresponding hydrocarbons. Density: bromo and iodo compounds are heavier than water. They are slightly soluble in water but soluble in organic solvents.

concepts.name: Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions of Haloalkanes – Introduction
concepts.explanation: In nucleophilic substitution, a nucleophile replaces the halogen atom (leaving group) in a haloalkane. The reaction is initiated by a nucleophile and is classified as Sₙ1 or Sₙ2 depending on the mechanism. R-X + Nu⁻ → R-Nu + X⁻.

concepts.name: Sₙ2 Reaction – Mechanism and Kinetics
concepts.explanation: Sₙ2 is a bimolecular nucleophilic substitution reaction (second order: rate ∝ [RX][Nu⁻]). It proceeds in a single step with backside attack, leading to inversion of configuration. Transition state has the nucleophile and leaving group partially bonded. Example: CH₃Cl + OH⁻ → CH₃OH + Cl⁻.

concepts.name: Sₙ2 Reaction – Steric Effects and Reactivity
concepts.explanation: The Sₙ2 reaction is hindered by bulky substituents on the carbon bearing the leaving group. Reactivity order: CH₃X > 1° > 2° > 3°. Methyl halides react fastest due to least steric hindrance; tertiary halides react very slowly (or not at all) via Sₙ2.

concepts.name: Sₙ1 Reaction – Mechanism and Kinetics
concepts.explanation: Sₙ1 is a unimolecular nucleophilic substitution reaction (first order: rate ∝ [RX]). It proceeds in two steps: (1) slow formation of carbocation (rate-determining); (2) fast attack of nucleophile on carbocation. It leads to racemisation. Example: (CH₃)₃CBr + OH⁻ → (CH₃)₃COH + Br⁻.

concepts.name: Sₙ1 Reaction – Carbocation Stability and Reactivity
concepts.explanation: Sₙ1 reactions are favoured by the stability of the carbocation intermediate. Reactivity order: 3° > 2° > 1° > CH₃X. Allylic and benzylic halides also react readily via Sₙ1 due to resonance stabilisation of the carbocation.

concepts.name: Sₙ1 vs Sₙ2 – Comparison and Factors
concepts.explanation: Sₙ2: one step, backside attack, inversion, no carbocation, 1° > 2° > 3°. Sₙ1: two steps, carbocation intermediate, racemisation, 3° > 2° > 1°. Sₙ1 favoured by polar protic solvents and stable carbocations; Sₙ2 favoured by polar aprotic solvents and less hindered substrates.

concepts.name: Chirality and Optical Activity in Haloalkanes
concepts.explanation: A carbon atom with four different groups is chiral (asymmetric). Chiral molecules exist as two non-superimposable mirror images (enantiomers). Enantiomers rotate plane-polarised light in opposite directions. A 50:50 mixture is a racemic mixture (optically inactive).

concepts.name: Sₙ2 and Inversion of Configuration
concepts.explanation: Sₙ2 reactions of chiral alkyl halides proceed with inversion of configuration (Walden inversion). The nucleophile attacks from the back side, and the leaving group departs from the front side. Example: (-)-2-bromobutane + OH⁻ → (+)-butan-2-ol.

concepts.name: Sₙ1 and Racemisation
concepts.explanation: Sₙ1 reactions of chiral alkyl halides proceed with racemisation. The planar carbocation intermediate is attacked by the nucleophile from either side, giving a mixture of enantiomers (racemic mixture). Example: optically active 2-bromobutane → (±)-butan-2-ol.

concepts.name: Elimination Reactions of Haloalkanes (β-Elimination)
concepts.explanation: Haloalkanes with β-hydrogens on treatment with alcoholic KOH undergo elimination of HX to form alkenes. This is β-elimination (dehydrohalogenation). Example: CH₃CH₂Br + alc.KOH → CH₂=CH₂ + KBr + H₂O.

concepts.name: Saytzeff's Rule (Zaitsev's Rule)
concepts.explanation: In dehydrohalogenation, the major product is the alkene with the greater number of alkyl groups attached to the doubly bonded carbon atoms (more substituted alkene). Example: 2-bromopentane gives pent-2-ene (major) rather than pent-1-ene.

concepts.name: Elimination vs Substitution – Competition
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides with β-hydrogens can undergo both substitution (Sₙ1/Sₙ2) and elimination. The outcome depends on: (i) nature of alkyl halide; (ii) strength and size of base/nucleophile; (iii) reaction conditions. Bulky bases favour elimination; good nucleophiles favour substitution.

concepts.name: Grignard Reagents – Preparation
concepts.explanation: Grignard reagents (RMgX) are prepared by reacting haloalkanes with magnesium metal in dry ether. Example: CH₃CH₂Br + Mg → CH₃CH₂MgBr (dry ether). The C-Mg bond is highly polar (Cδ⁻–Mgδ⁺), making the carbon nucleophilic.

concepts.name: Grignard Reagents – Reactions with Protic Compounds
concepts.explanation: Grignard reagents react with compounds containing acidic hydrogens (H₂O, alcohols, amines, acids) to form hydrocarbons. Thus, Grignard reagents must be prepared and handled under anhydrous conditions. Example: RMgX + H₂O → RH + Mg(OH)X.

concepts.name: Wurtz Reaction
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides react with sodium metal in dry ether to give alkanes with double the number of carbon atoms. Example: 2CH₃Br + 2Na → CH₃–CH₃ + 2NaBr (dry ether).

concepts.name: Reactivity of Haloarenes in Nucleophilic Substitution
concepts.explanation: Haloarenes are much less reactive than haloalkanes towards nucleophilic substitution due to: (i) resonance (C-X bond has partial double bond character); (ii) sp² hybridisation of carbon (more electronegative, stronger bond); (iii) instability of phenyl cation; (iv) electron-rich arene repels nucleophiles.

concepts.name: Nucleophilic Substitution in Haloarenes – Effect of Electron Withdrawing Groups
concepts.explanation: Presence of electron withdrawing groups (e.g., -NO₂) at ortho and para positions increases the reactivity of haloarenes towards nucleophilic substitution. The carbanion intermediate is stabilised by resonance with the nitro group. Meta-substituted haloarenes show no such activation.

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution in Haloarenes
concepts.explanation: Haloarenes undergo electrophilic substitution reactions (halogenation, nitration, sulphonation, Friedel-Crafts). The halogen atom is o,p-directing but deactivating (due to resonance donation counteracting inductive withdrawal). Conditions are more drastic than for benzene.

concepts.name: Wurtz-Fittig Reaction
concepts.explanation: A mixture of an alkyl halide and an aryl halide reacts with sodium metal in dry ether to give alkylarenes. Example: C₆H₅Br + CH₃Br + 2Na → C₆H₅CH₃ + 2NaBr.

concepts.name: Fittig Reaction
concepts.explanation: Aryl halides react with sodium metal in dry ether to give biaryls (diaryl compounds). Example: 2C₆H₅Br + 2Na → C₆H₅–C₆H₅ + 2NaBr.

concepts.name: Dichloromethane (Methylene Chloride) – Uses and Hazards
concepts.explanation: CH₂Cl₂ is a colourless liquid used as a solvent (paint remover, aerosol propellant, metal cleaner). Inhalation can cause CNS depression, dizziness, and nausea. Exposure affects hearing and vision.

concepts.name: Chloroform (Trichloromethane) – Uses and Hazards
concepts.explanation: CHCl₃ is a colourless liquid used as a solvent and formerly as an anaesthetic. It is oxidised by air in light to phosgene (COCl₂), a poisonous gas. Stored in dark bottles. Exposure affects the liver and kidneys.

concepts.name: Iodoform (Triiodomethane) – Uses
concepts.explanation: CHI₃ is a yellow solid used as an antiseptic (due to iodine release). Its antiseptic properties are due to free iodine, not iodoform itself. Has an objectionable smell.

concepts.name: Carbon Tetrachloride (Tetrachloromethane) – Uses and Hazards
concepts.explanation: CCl₄ is a colourless liquid used as a solvent, degreasing agent, and in fire extinguishers (now phased out). Exposure causes dizziness, nausea, and liver damage. It depletes the ozone layer.

concepts.name: Freons (Chlorofluorocarbons – CFCs)
concepts.explanation: Freons are chlorofluorocarbons of methane and ethane (e.g., Freon-12: CCl₂F₂). They are stable, non-toxic, non-corrosive, and easily liquefiable gases. Used as refrigerants and aerosol propellants. They cause ozone layer depletion through radical chain reactions.

concepts.name: DDT (p,p'-Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane)
concepts.explanation: DDT is a chlorinated organic insecticide. It is chemically stable and fat-soluble, leading to bioaccumulation and biomagnification. It was banned in the US in 1973 but is still used in some parts of the world due to its effectiveness against malaria-carrying mosquitoes.
